readings: Let Flowers of Many Kinds Blossom, Diverse Schools of Thought Contend!

readingAnalyses.title: Let Flowers of Many Kinds Blossom, Diverse Schools of Thought Contend!
readingAnalyses.author: Lu Ting-yi
readingAnalyses.source: Speech by Director of the Propaganda Department of the CCP Central Committee, May 26, 1956
readingAnalyses.summary: This is the founding document of the Hundred Flowers Campaign, delivered by Lu Ting-yi announcing the Party's new policy toward intellectuals. The speech promises artists, writers, and scientists unprecedented freedom to debate, criticize, and express diverse opinions. However, it immediately establishes the crucial caveat: this freedom applies only 'among the people' - counter-revolutionaries receive only dictatorship. The speech reveals the Party's attempt to distinguish between legitimate 'comradely criticism' and dangerous dissent, while calling for unity with non-Party intellectuals and an end to sectarianism among Communist cadres.

keyQuotes.quote: To artists and writers, we say, 'Let flowers of many kinds blossom.' To scientists we say, 'Let diverse schools of thought contend.'

The freedom we uphold is not the same as that based on the type of democracy advocated by the bourgeoisie... We, on the contrary, hold that there must be democratic liberties among the people, but that no freedom should be extended to counter-revolutionaries: for them we have only dictatorship.

Among the people themselves there is freedom not only to spread materialism but also to propagate idealism. Provided he is not a counter-revolutionary, everyone is free to expound materialism or idealism.

There are two kinds of criticism. One is criticism directed against the enemy - what people call criticism that 'kills at a blow,' criticism with no holds barred. The other is criticism directed against the honestly mistaken - well-meant, comradely criticism, made in the cause of unity.
It is easy to make mistakes. But mistakes should be rectified immediately, the sooner the better. It is sticking to one's mistakes that does the harm.

thematicExamples: Lu's contrast between 'bourgeois freedom' (citing the Rosenbergs' execution in America) and 'people's freedom' reveals how the Party justified limited liberalization
The call to end 'sectarianism' among Party members - some Communists had become 'swelled-headed' after victory and monopolized positions

The distinction between ideological struggle 'among the people' (debate to achieve unity) and struggle against 'counter-revolutionaries' (suppression) - the crucial boundary that would later trap critics
The insistence on learning from the Soviet Union while not 'mechanically copying' - showing early signs of Sino-Soviet tensions
realLifeConnections: The boundary between 'people' and 'counter-revolutionaries' mirrors modern authoritarian tactics - inviting criticism while reserving the right to reclassify critics as enemies
The promise of intellectual freedom within political limits parallels debates about academic freedom in contemporary universities
Lu's warning against 'sectarianism' and monopolizing positions resonates with critiques of ideological capture in institutions today
The tension between 'unity through criticism' and 'dictatorship over enemies' illuminates how regimes maintain control while performing openness

overallSynthesis:
Lu Ting-yi's speech is a masterpiece of authoritarian doublespeak that rewards close reading. On the surface, it promises intellectuals exactly what they wanted: freedom to debate, criticize, and think independently. The classical allusion 'Let a hundred flowers bloom, let a hundred schools of thought contend' (drawn from the vibrant intellectual culture of the Warring States period) suggests a return to China's golden age of philosophical diversity.

But embedded throughout are the categories that would doom those who trusted this invitation. The speech insists on distinguishing between 'the people' (who deserve freedom) and 'counter-revolutionaries' (who deserve dictatorship). The problem is that the Party alone decides who belongs in which category - and the boundaries can shift overnight. Critics who spoke in 1956 thinking they were exercising 'freedom among the people' found themselves reclassified as 'rightists' by 1957.

The speech also reveals the Party's instrumental view of intellectual freedom. The goal is not liberalism but national strength: 'If we want our country to be prosperous and strong, we must... have a flourishing art, literature and science.' Freedom is granted because it is useful, not because it is a right. When it became inconvenient - when criticism exceeded acceptable bounds - it was revoked. Over 550,000 intellectuals were labeled 'rightists' in the Anti-Rightist Campaign, their careers and lives destroyed. Lu Ting-yi himself was later purged during the Cultural Revolution.
executiveSummary: Lu Ting-yi's May 1956 speech launched the Hundred Flowers Campaign, inviting intellectuals to criticize the Party with the promise: 'Let flowers of many kinds blossom, diverse schools of thought contend.' The speech promises freedom of debate among 'the people' while reserving 'dictatorship' for 'counter-revolutionaries.' This category distinction proved fatal: when criticism exceeded expectations, critics were reclassified as enemies. The Anti-Rightist Campaign (1957) destroyed over 550,000 intellectuals. The reading reveals how authoritarian regimes perform openness while maintaining the power to punish dissent - a template that echoes through Mao's later campaigns and resonates with contemporary authoritarian tactics.

keywords.definition: 1956-57 invitation to intellectuals to criticize the Party, followed by brutal crackdown
keywords.relevance: Established pattern of mobilization-then-repression that defined Maoist rule

keywords.definition: 1957 campaign that labeled 550,000+ intellectuals as 'rightists'; careers destroyed, many sent to labor camps
keywords.relevance: Created a generation of silenced intellectuals; trauma shapes Chinese society today

keywords.definition: The Party's distinction between those deserving freedom and those deserving dictatorship
keywords.relevance: The key category that enabled repression: the Party decides who belongs where

keywords.definition: Lu's criticism of Party members who monopolized positions and excluded non-Party intellectuals
keywords.relevance: Shows Party's attempt to mobilize broader support - but on Party terms

keywords.definition: Leninist principle: debate within the Party, unity after decisions made
keywords.relevance: The speech extends debate beyond the Party but keeps the Party as final arbiter

keyPassages.quote: Let flowers of many kinds blossom, diverse schools of thought contend

We must have democratic liberties among the people, but no freedom should be extended to counter-revolutionaries: for them we have only dictatorship. This is a question of drawing a political demarcation line.
When it comes to questions of ideas, administrative measures will get us nowhere. Only through open debate can materialism gradually conquer idealism.

people: Lu Ting-yi - Director of CCP Propaganda Department; delivered this speech; later purged in Cultural Revolution
Mao Zedong - Announced the policy at Supreme State Conference; would later reverse course brutally
Kuo Mo-jo - President of Chinese Academy of Sciences; asked Lu to give this speech
places: Beijing - Capital where intellectuals gathered and later were persecuted
Soviet Union - Model for learning; Lu warns against 'mechanical copying'
United States - Contrasted negatively ('jingos,' Rosenbergs' execution)
things: Hundred Flowers Campaign - Brief opening (1956-57); then trap snapped shut
Anti-Rightist Campaign - 550,000+ labeled; careers destroyed; labor camps
People's Dictatorship - Freedom for 'people,' suppression for 'counter-revolutionaries'

keyQuestions.question: Lu says freedom is for 'the people' but dictatorship for 'counter-revolutionaries.' Who decides which category you belong to?
keyQuestions.talkingPoints: The Party decides. And categories can shift: critics who spoke in 1956 as 'people' became 'rightists' in 1957. The distinction sounds principled but enables arbitrary repression. Compare to other regimes that distinguish 'legitimate' from 'illegitimate' dissent.

keyQuestions.question: Why would intellectuals trust this invitation? And why did Mao issue it?
keyQuestions.talkingPoints: Scholars debate whether it was genuine (Mao wanted feedback but recoiled at its intensity) or a trap (identify enemies by letting them reveal themselves). The speech itself is ambiguous. The result was traumatic either way.

keyQuestions.question: Lu criticizes 'sectarianism' among Party members. What does this reveal about tensions within the CCP?
keyQuestions.talkingPoints: Some Party members had become arrogant after victory, monopolizing positions and excluding non-Party talent. Lu wants to mobilize broader support. But the Party retains ultimate authority. Compare to contemporary debates about ideological gatekeeping.

timeline.event: Khrushchev's 'Secret Speech' denounces Stalin; shocks communist world
Intellectuals criticize Party; criticism exceeds expectations
Mao reverses course; Anti-Rightist Campaign begins
550,000+ labeled 'rightists'; sent to labor camps or rural exile
Lu Ting-yi himself purged during Cultural Revolution

connections.fromPrevious: Plate 22 showed Japan rebuilding under American occupation with new democratic constitution. China took a radically different path - revolutionary transformation under Party dictatorship, with 'democracy' redefined as freedom for allies and suppression for enemies.
connections.toNext: Plate 24 examines Korea's division. The contrast between North (following Mao's model) and South illuminates what Maoist 'people's democracy' meant in practice - and its alternatives.
connections.broaderThemes: The Hundred Flowers reveals the central tension of revolutionary regimes: they need intellectuals for development but fear independent thought. This tension recurs across the course: Meiji reformers vs. traditionalists, colonial modernizers vs. nationalists, post-war authoritarian developmentalism.

nextPreview.preview: Korea's liberation from Japan (1945) led immediately to division and war (1950-53). Two short stories - 'Cranes' (South) and 'Five Bandits' (South, critical) - reveal how Koreans experienced division differently.

teachingNotes: READ THE SPEECH CLOSELY - Lu's language reveals the trap. 'Freedom for the people, dictatorship for counter-revolutionaries' sounds principled until you ask: who decides?
The Rosenberg reference is striking: Lu contrasts American 'bourgeois freedom' (which executed the Rosenbergs) with Chinese 'people's freedom.' Cold War context matters.
Lu himself was later purged - the revolution devours its own. Mention this for dramatic irony.
Contemporary relevance: Xi Jinping has revived some Maoist language. How do authoritarian regimes perform openness while maintaining control?
Ask students: If you were a Chinese intellectual in 1956, would you have trusted this invitation? What signals would you look for?
